items.title: E-commerce order export (CSV)
items.scenario: A startup receives `orders.csv` (2M rows) from their warehouse with columns `order_id`, `customer_id`, `amount`, `created_at`, and `is_return`. The ML engineer only needs four columns for a return-prediction baseline.
items.explanation: They call `read_csv` with `usecols` and explicit dtypes: `order_id` as string (not float), `customer_id` as category, `amount` as float32, `is_return` as int8, and `parse_dates=["created_at"]`. Memory drops from ~1.2 GB to ~180 MB compared to default inference.

items.title: Feature store snapshot (Parquet)
items.scenario: A recommendation team stores daily user embeddings in partitioned Parquet under `features/date=2024-01-15/`. Training needs only `user_id` and `embedding` for one date partition.
items.explanation: `pd.read_parquet(path, columns=["user_id", "embedding"])` reads just those columns with native types—no string parsing, no full-table scan of unused fields.

items.title: API event logs (JSON Lines)
items.scenario: Mobile clickstream logs arrive as `.jsonl` with nested `properties` objects. Analysts flatten to a tabular model for fraud detection.
items.explanation: They load with `pd.read_json("events.jsonl", lines=True)`, then `pd.json_normalize` on nested fields. They assign `dtype={"event_type": "category"}` early so repeated string values do not sit in object columns.

items.title: Time-ordered sales before splitting
items.scenario: A demand-forecasting project loads 5 years of daily sales. The team must not shuffle rows at load because the validation period is the last 90 days.
items.explanation: After `read_parquet`, they sort by `date` and record that split boundaries are index-based on sorted rows—not random sampling at load. Global mean imputation on the full frame would leak future sales into past rows.
